_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Williamson County, TN. Formed 1799. Records survive from formation despite Civil War combat in the county. The Battle of Franklin (1864) did not destroy the courthouse, and the county's wealth ensured good record-keeping.

current_courthouse.location: Franklin, TN — Public Square (historic courthouse, now also administrative offices)

previous_courthouses.fate: Early frame courthouse. Replaced as the county grew.
Brick courthouse. Served the county through its period of greatest antebellum prosperity. Replaced by the current 1858 building.

record_losses.cause: Civil War occupation and Battle of Franklin — minor disruption, no major record destruction
record_losses.description: Despite the devastating Battle of Franklin (November 30, 1864) and subsequent Federal occupation, the Williamson County courthouse and its records survived intact. The battle was fought south of the town center, and while buildings in Franklin were used as field hospitals, the courthouse was not destroyed. Some court operations were disrupted during the war years, creating gaps in the minute book sequence, but the bound record books survived. Williamson County's antebellum wealth ensured thorough record-keeping, and the postwar Reconstruction-era records are also well-preserved.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The Battle of Franklin devastated Williamson County's people but not its records — {ancestor_name}'s deeds, wills, and court appearances survive in an unbroken series from 1799. The courthouse that held these records stood while the town around it became a battlefield.
record_losses.source: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records. Jacobson and Rupp, For Cause and for Country.

deeds.gaps: No significant gaps known. The antebellum deed books document the county's plantation economy in detail.
deeds.repository: Williamson County Register of Deeds; Tennessee State Library and Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com Tennessee land records

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — wills, inventories, and estate settlements from the earliest period
wills_estates.gaps: Minor gaps in loose estate papers. Bound volumes are complete. Estate inventories are particularly valuable — they include enslaved people by name (sometimes) or by age/sex.
wills_estates.repository: Williamson County Probate Court; Tennessee State Library and Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; limited TSLA digital access

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds from formation; licenses from 1838
marriages.gaps: Marriage bonds are the primary pre-1838 record. Some gaps in the earliest years.
marriages.repository: Williamson County Clerk; Tennessee State Library and Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good — some disruption during Civil War years but bound volumes survived
court_minutes.gaps: War-era gaps in regular court sessions (1862-1865) reflect disruption of government rather than record loss.
court_minutes.repository: Tennessee State Library and Archives; Williamson County Archives

vital_records.completeness: Tennessee statewide registration began in 1908. Compliance was incomplete in rural areas until the 1920s.
vital_records.repository: Tennessee Department of Health (Office of Vital Records)

substitute_sources.description: Williamson County's high enslaved population makes the slave schedules particularly important. The 1850 and 1860 slave schedules list slaveholders with age, sex, and color of each enslaved person (but not names).
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for identifying enslaved families. Cross-referencing slave schedules with estate records (which sometimes name enslaved people) can connect unnamed schedule entries to individuals. The 1870 census then provides names, closing the gap.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives. Federal Census Slave Schedules, Williamson County, TN.

substitute_sources.source_type: Battle of Franklin records and rosters
substitute_sources.description: Extensive military records from the Battle of Franklin document Confederate and Federal casualties, including names, units, wounds, and burial locations. The Confederate cemetery at Carnton plantation has been thoroughly documented.
substitute_sources.repository: Tennessee State Library and Archives; National Archives (Confederate compiled service records); Carnton Association archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: If an ancestor served in a unit engaged at Franklin, the battle records provide detail unavailable in standard compiled service records — hospital records, burial registers, and eyewitness accounts.
substitute_sources.source: Jacobson, Eric A., and Richard A. Rupp. For Cause and for Country. O'More Publishing, 2006.

substitute_sources.description: Williamson County's churches maintained membership rolls, baptism records, and session minutes. Presbyterian churches (reflecting the Scots-Irish planter class) and Methodist churches are well-represented.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Tennessee State Library and Archives; Historical Foundation of the Presbyterian and Reformed Churches
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records supplement civil records, particularly for marriages before 1838 and for vital events before 1908. Session records provide biographical detail absent from civil records.
substitute_sources.source: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to Church Records in the TSLA.

narrative_hooks: The Battle of Franklin killed nearly 7,000 men in five hours, but it did not destroy the Williamson County courthouse — {ancestor_name}'s deeds, wills, and court records survived the worst day in the county's history.

Williamson County's estate inventories are among the richest in Tennessee — if {ancestor_name} enslaved people, the inventory may name them. If {ancestor_name} was enslaved, the inventory may be the first record that proves {pronoun_subject} existed.
Before 1838, Tennessee did not require marriage licenses, so a missing marriage record for {ancestor_name} in Williamson County is normal — look for the marriage bond in the County Clerk's records.
Tennessee vital registration began in 1908, so the absence of a birth certificate for {ancestor_name} before that date is standard across the state — not a gap, just the era.
source_references: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records in the Tennessee State Library and Archives. Nashville: TSLA.
Jacobson, Eric A., and Richard A. Rupp. For Cause and for Country. Franklin: O'More Publishing, 2006.
Sistler, Byron, and Barbara Sistler. Early Middle Tennessee Marriages. Nashville: Byron Sistler & Associates, 1988.
